name: Cow milk, pasteurized, vitamin A + D added, 0% fat (food medium)
description: Food-derived medium: metabolizable components of Cow milk, pasteurized, vitamin A + D added, 0% fat [Milk and milk products] from FooDB, plus a standard M9 mineral base. Organic components use presence-based uptake bounds; FooDB content retained. Measured compounds with no BiGG exchange are listed under uncovered (capped at 250).

provenance.citation: FooDB v2020-04-07 (Wishart DS et al., foodb.ca). Content sources: MANUAL, USDA.
provenance.notes: Organic components mapped from FooDB compounds to BiGG via InChIKey/name. Mineral base added. Bounds presence-based; foodb_content retained. Unmapped measured compounds kept in uncovered (v2 completeness fix). IMPORTANT: composition values are taken from the archived FooDB 2020-04-07 CSV data release; FooDB's live food page no longer displays this bulk composition, so it will appear empty. Verify individual compounds via the per-component cross-reference links. Underlying content was aggregated by FooDB from USDA / DTU / Phenol Explorer.
provenance.verification: expert-curated (multi-source food composition; growth-relevant profile completed, FooDB noise removed)
curation_sources: USDA FoodData Central FDC 168935 -- Milk, nonfat, fluid, with added vitamin A and vitamin D (fat free or skim): full proximate/mineral/vitamin/amino-acid panel per 100 g
Fox, McSweeney, Cogan & Guinee, Dairy Chemistry and Biochemistry, 4th ed., Springer 2015 -- citrate (~1.75 g/L), chloride, minor sugars, minerals, carnitine, inositol
Belitz, Grosch & Schieberle, Food Chemistry -- milk organic-acid profile (citric as major acid)
Urbiene S. & Leskauskaite D. 2006, Pol. J. Food Nutr. Sci. 15/56(3):277-281 -- milk organic/nucleic acids incl. orotate and hippurate

Reversed-phase HPLC dairy organic-acid profiles (Lee et al. 1995 Korean J. Dairy Sci.; Fernandez-Garcia & McGregor 1994 J. Dairy Sci. 77:2934) -- lactate/pyruvate/succinate/hippurate/orotate quantities
provenance.curation_notes: Dominant carbon/energy source is LACTOSE (~4.9 g/100g, ~145 mM); the second, growth-relevant carbon source is CITRATE (~0.175 g/100g, ~9 mM) which was entirely missing from the FooDB list. Nitrogen is supplied by milk protein/amino acids (3.37 g/100g protein, casein-dominant) plus ~0.025 g/100g urea. Because this item is 0% fat, fat-soluble vitamins (E, K) and the MFGM phospholipids (PC/SM species) are near-zero. Removed FooDB noise: toxic/non-nutritive trace metals (arsenate, cadmium, aluminium
curation_added: citric acid (citrate) ~0.175 g/100g -- THE major organic acid of milk, absent from the FooDB list (key growth-relevant carbon source and Ca/Mg chelator)
orotic acid (orotate) ~7 mg/100g -- characteristic milk pyrimidine metabolite / LAB growth factor
explicit lactose amount (~4.9 g/100g) -- dominant carbon/energy source, quantified
chloride (~100 mg/100g) and quantified mineral/vitamin panel

curation_removed.name: Arsenate
curation_removed.reason: Non-nutritive toxic trace contaminant; not a growth nutrient of milk.

curation_removed.name: Cadmium
curation_removed.reason: Non-nutritive heavy-metal contaminant.

curation_removed.name: Aluminum
curation_removed.reason: Non-nutritive trace contaminant; not an essential nutrient.

curation_removed.name: Rubidium
curation_removed.reason: Non-nutritive trace element; incidental, not a growth nutrient.

curation_removed.name: Boron
curation_removed.reason: Non-essential ultratrace element; not a defining milk growth nutrient.

curation_removed.name: Nickel
curation_removed.reason: Non-essential ultratrace contaminant for this medium.

curation_removed.name: Methanol
curation_removed.reason: Trace volatile / FooDB artifact; not a milk growth nutrient.

curation_removed.name: Acetone
curation_removed.reason: Trace volatile ketone (blood-derived); not a growth nutrient at milk levels.

curation_removed.name: D-Ornithine
curation_removed.reason: D-enantiomer is a FooDB stereochemistry artifact; milk ornithine is the L-form and only trace.

curation_removed.name: D-Serine
curation_removed.reason: D-enantiomer is a FooDB artifact; replaced by protein-derived L-serine.

curation_removed.name: Iron (Fe3+)
curation_removed.reason: Redundant redox-state duplicate of iron; consolidated into single 'iron' entry.

curation_removed.name: Fe2+ mitochondria
curation_removed.reason: FooDB subcellular-compartment artifact; consolidated into single 'iron' entry.

curation_removed.name: Co2+
curation_removed.reason: Free cobalt ion is a FooDB artifact; milk's biologically relevant cobalt is delivered as cobalamin (B12), which is retained.

curation_removed.name: L Kynurenine C10H12N2O3
curation_removed.reason: Trace tryptophan-catabolism metabolite; not a growth-relevant milk nutrient.

curation_removed.name: Butyryl carnitine
curation_removed.reason: Trace acylcarnitine metabolite; not a defined growth nutrient (free L-carnitine retained).

curation_removed.name: Propionyl-carnitine
curation_removed.reason: Trace acylcarnitine metabolite; not a defined growth nutrient.

curation_removed.name: Tetradecanoyl carnitine
curation_removed.reason: Trace acylcarnitine metabolite; not a defined growth nutrient.

curation_removed.name: L-Acetylcarnitine
curation_removed.reason: Trace acylcarnitine metabolite; not a defined growth nutrient.

curation_removed.name: N-acetyl-L-cysteine
curation_removed.reason: Not a genuine free milk constituent at nutritional level; FooDB noise.

curation_removed.name: 3-Methylbutanoic acid
curation_removed.reason: Trace flavor volatile; not a growth nutrient.

curation_removed.name: D-Galactarate
curation_removed.reason: Implausible/predicted oxidation product; not a measured milk nutrient.

components.name: lactose
components.evidence_source: USDA FDC 168935 (total sugars 5.09 g/100g, ~all lactose in skim milk); Fox & McSweeney, Dairy Chemistry & Biochemistry 4th ed. 2015

components.name: D-galactose
components.evidence_source: Fox & McSweeney 2015; USDA lists it within total sugars

components.name: citric acid
components.evidence_source: Fox & McSweeney, Dairy Chemistry & Biochemistry 2015; Belitz & Grosch, Food Chemistry (1.75 g/L typical, range 1.32-2.1 g/L)

components.name: urea
components.evidence_source: Fox & McSweeney 2015 (milk urea nitrogen ~10-14 mg/dL -> urea ~250 mg/L)

components.name: chloride
components.evidence_source: Fox & McSweeney 2015 (milk chloride ~1.0 g/L); not in USDA panel

components.name: Levoglutamide (glutamine), 2-Oxopentanedioic acid (2-oxoglutarate), Propanedioic acid (malonate) -- genuine trace metabolites without clean growth-nutrient amounts

uncovered.name: orotic acid
uncovered.note: no exchange auto-assigned; propose uptake -1.0 pending manual curation

uncovered.name: hippuric acid
uncovered.note: no exchange auto-assigned; propose uptake -1.0 pending manual curation

uncovered.name: creatine
uncovered.note: no exchange auto-assigned; propose uptake -1.0 pending manual curation

uncovered.name: PC(...) diacyl/plasmalogen phosphatidylcholines and SM(d18:0/16:0) -- genuine milk-fat-globule-membrane phospholipids, but this is 0% FAT skim milk so they are removed with the fat and are near-negligible; no BiGG metabolite anyway
uncovered.note: no exchange auto-assigned; propose uptake -1.0 pending manual curation

oxygen_note: food substrate; O2 availability is a simulation condition, not a property of the food
basis: per 100 g fluid milk (approx. per 100 mL; density ~1.033 g/mL)
